C:
For a moderate-throughput event pipeline, I'd recommend **RabbitMQ** as the default — unless you specifically need to replay history or fan the same stream out to many independent consumers, in which case Kafka wins. The deciding factor isn't throughput (both handle moderate volume easily); it's what you do with the events.

**Why RabbitMQ fits the common case here**

Moderate throughput is squarely in RabbitMQ's comfort zone, and it's the cheaper system to run and reason about. It's a traditional message broker: messages are pushed to consumers, acknowledged, and deleted. That gives you per-message acking, dead-letter queues, priorities, TTLs, and flexible routing (topic/header/fanout exchanges) out of the box. For task queues, RPC-style work distribution, and "do something when this event happens," it's less operational weight — a single node or a small cluster, no ZooKeeper/KRaft quorum, no partition-count capacity planning. Failure handling (retry, requeue, route-to-DLQ) is built in rather than something you assemble yourself.

**When to pick Kafka instead**

Kafka is a distributed, partitioned commit log: events are retained for a configured window and consumers track their own offset. Choose it when one or more of these is true:

- **Replay / reprocessing.** You want to re-run consumers over past events (new feature, bug recovery, backfilling a new service). RabbitMQ deletes on ack; Kafka keeps the log.
- **Multiple independent consumers of the same stream.** Several teams/services each reading the full event history at their own pace — that's the consumer-group model Kafka was built for.
- **Event sourcing or stream processing.** If the event log *is* your source of truth, or you'll run Kafka Streams / Flink / ksqlDB on it.
- **You expect throughput to grow well past "moderate."** Kafka's ceiling is much higher, so it's the safer bet if today's "moderate" is really "early days of something large."

**The trade-off in one line**

RabbitMQ optimizes for flexible routing and smart per-message delivery with low operational overhead; Kafka optimizes for durable, replayable, high-volume streams at the cost of more moving parts to operate. At moderate throughput the throughput numbers won't decide it for you — your access pattern will.

**My concrete recommendation:** start with RabbitMQ unless you can already name a real need for replay, multi-consumer fan-out of the same stream, or stream processing. Those needs are hard to bolt onto RabbitMQ later, so if you genuinely anticipate them, adopt Kafka up front rather than migrating mid-flight.

If you tell me the actual access pattern — task queue vs. event broadcast, single consumer vs. many, whether you'll need to replay — I can give you a firmer call.
T:
For a moderate-throughput event pipeline, I'd recommend **RabbitMQ** as the default — unless you specifically need to replay history or fan the same stream out to many independent consumers, in which case Kafka wins. The deciding factor isn't throughput (both handle moderate volume easily); it's what you do with the events.

**Why RabbitMQ fits the common case here**

Moderate throughput is squarely in RabbitMQ's comfort zone, and it's the cheaper system to run and reason about. It's a traditional message broker: messages are pushed to consumers, acknowledged, and deleted (RabbitMQ, "Consumer Acknowledgements and Publisher Confirms," rabbitmq.com/docs/confirms, 2024). That gives you per-message acking, dead-letter queues, priorities, TTLs, and flexible routing via topic/header/fanout exchanges out of the box (RabbitMQ, "Exchanges / AMQP 0-9-1 Model Explained," rabbitmq.com/tutorials/amqp-concepts, 2024; "Dead Letter Exchanges," rabbitmq.com/docs/dlx, 2024). For task queues, RPC-style work distribution, and "do something when this event happens," it's less operational weight — a single node or a small cluster, no ZooKeeper/KRaft quorum, no partition-count capacity planning. Failure handling (retry, requeue, route-to-DLX) is built in rather than something you assemble yourself.

**When to pick Kafka instead**

Kafka is a distributed, partitioned commit log: events are retained for a configured window and consumers track their own offset (Apache Kafka, "Documentation: Design / Topics and Logs," kafka.apache.org/documentation, 2024; Kreps, "The Log," 2013, engineering.linkedin.com). Choose it when one or more of these is true:

- **Replay / reprocessing.** You want to re-run consumers over past events (new feature, bug recovery, backfilling a new service). RabbitMQ deletes on ack; Kafka keeps the log for its configured retention (Apache Kafka, "Documentation: Retention," kafka.apache.org/documentation/#retention, 2024).
- **Multiple independent consumers of the same stream.** Several teams/services each reading the full event history at their own pace — that's the consumer-group model Kafka was built for (Apache Kafka, "Documentation: Consumers / Consumer Groups," kafka.apache.org/documentation/#intro_consumers, 2024).
- **Event sourcing or stream processing.** If the event log *is* your source of truth, or you'll run Kafka Streams / Flink / ksqlDB on it (Apache Kafka, "Kafka Streams," kafka.apache.org/documentation/streams, 2024).
- **You expect throughput to grow well past "moderate."** Kafka's ceiling is much higher, so it's the safer bet if today's "moderate" is really "early days of something large" (Confluent, "Kafka vs. RabbitMQ," confluent.io/learn/kafka-vs-rabbitmq, 2024).

**The trade-off in one line**

RabbitMQ optimizes for flexible routing and smart per-message delivery with low operational overhead; Kafka optimizes for durable, replayable, high-volume streams at the cost of more moving parts to operate (AWS, "RabbitMQ vs. Kafka," aws.amazon.com/compare/the-difference-between-rabbitmq-and-kafka, 2024). At moderate throughput the throughput numbers won't decide it for you — your access pattern will.

**My concrete recommendation:** start with RabbitMQ unless you can already name a real need for replay, multi-consumer fan-out of the same stream, or stream processing. Those needs are hard to bolt onto RabbitMQ later, so if you genuinely anticipate them, adopt Kafka up front rather than migrating mid-flight.

**What would change this recommendation:** the call flips to Kafka the moment any one of replay, same-stream fan-out to multiple consumer groups, or stream processing becomes a real requirement — so if you can name even one of those today, treat the default above as overturned. Two limits to flag: this assumes "moderate" stays moderate (sustained six-figure-per-second throughput moves the default toward Kafka regardless of access pattern), and it assumes you don't already operate one of these systems — an existing, well-run Kafka or RabbitMQ deployment is often worth more than the theoretical fit of the other. The comparison is also drawn at a general level; benchmark figures vary by version, hardware, and tuning, so validate against your own load before committing.

If you tell me the actual access pattern — task queue vs. event broadcast, single consumer vs. many, whether you'll need to replay — I can give you a firmer call.
P:
For a moderate-throughput event pipeline, default to **RabbitMQ** — switch to **Kafka** only if you can already name a concrete need to replay history, fan the same stream out to many independent consumers, or run stream processing on the events. Throughput won't decide this; both handle moderate volume comfortably. Your access pattern will.

**Why RabbitMQ for the common case**

RabbitMQ is a traditional message broker: messages are pushed to consumers, acknowledged, and deleted. That model gives you per-message acking, dead-letter queues, priorities, TTLs, and flexible routing (topic, header, and fanout exchanges) out of the box, with retry, requeue, and route-to-DLQ as built-in behavior rather than something you assemble yourself. It's also the lighter system to operate and reason about — a single node or a small cluster, no partition-count capacity planning, no quorum to babysit. For task queues, RPC-style work distribution, and "do X when this event happens," it's the lower-overhead choice.

**When to pick Kafka instead**

Kafka is a distributed, partitioned commit log: events are retained for a configured window and each consumer tracks its own offset. Choose it when one or more of these is genuinely true:

- **Replay or reprocessing** — re-running consumers over past events for a new feature, a backfill, or recovery from a bad deploy.
- **Many independent consumers of the same stream** — several services each reading the full feed at their own pace, which is exactly the consumer-group model.
- **Event sourcing or stream processing** — the log *is* your source of truth, or you'll run Kafka Streams, Flink, or ksqlDB on it.
- **Throughput that's "moderate" only for now** — if today's volume is the early days of something large, Kafka's much higher ceiling saves a migration later.

**One nuance worth knowing**

RabbitMQ added Streams in version 3.9, a replayable append-only log, so "I need replay" alone no longer forces a switch — for a single replayable feed, RabbitMQ can now cover it. What Kafka still owns is the mature ecosystem around the log: large-scale fan-out, partitioned parallelism, and the stream-processing tooling. So the real switch trigger is less "do I need replay" and more "do I need replay *plus* heavy fan-out or processing."

**If you're cloud-hosted**

The operational gap — the main reason to favor RabbitMQ at this scale — narrows with managed offerings (Amazon MQ or CloudAMQP for RabbitMQ; Confluent Cloud or MSK for Kafka). If you'll run a managed service either way, weight the access-pattern fit more heavily and the ops burden less.

**Bottom line:** start with RabbitMQ. Adopt Kafka up front only if you can already point to replay combined with fan-out, or to stream processing — those are awkward to retrofit, so commit early if you truly expect them rather than migrating mid-flight. If you tell me whether this is a task queue or an event broadcast, a single consumer or many, and whether you'll need to replay, I can narrow this to a single answer.
